Home Careers CV Builder Blog Pricing Contact Us

Frontend Developer Roadmap

Frontend Developer learners can use the Frontend Engineer roadmap as the closest Career2Day path for skills, projects, interview preparation, quiz practice and CV guidance.

Frontend Engineers own the user-facing layer of software products. They combine React, browser fundamentals, design systems, API integration, accessibility, performance, and product judgment.

  1. Home
  2. Careers
  3. Frontend Engineer

Core skills

  • Semantic HTML
  • CSS layout
  • JavaScript
  • accessibility
  • responsive design
  • state management
  • API integration
  • performance

Detailed skill guide

core skills

Semantic HTML

Use headings, landmarks, labels, links, buttons, and form controls so the browser gives users structure and behavior before JavaScript adds anything.

Why it matters: Good HTML is the base of accessibility, SEO, form behavior, and maintainable UI. It also reduces the amount of custom code needed later.

CSS layout

Use Flexbox, Grid, spacing systems, responsive constraints, and component-level styles to make screens adapt without fragile pixel guessing.

Why it matters: Most frontend bugs are visual regressions, overflow, inconsistent spacing, or layouts that work on one screen size only.

JavaScript

Handle events, arrays, objects, async requests, modules, browser APIs, and small state transitions clearly before adding framework complexity.

Why it matters: React still runs on JavaScript. Debugging props, effects, API responses, and user interactions is much easier when the language fundamentals are solid.

Accessibility

Build interfaces that work with keyboard navigation, screen readers, visible focus, labels, contrast, error messages, and predictable interaction patterns.

Why it matters: Accessible UI protects users, improves quality, and is a strong hiring signal because it shows product maturity rather than visual-only implementation.

technical skills

Responsive design

Design layouts that reflow intentionally across phones, tablets, laptops, and wide monitors using stable dimensions and content-aware breakpoints.

Why it matters: A frontend engineer is responsible for real usage conditions, not just the screenshot that looked good on their own laptop.

State management

Keep UI state predictable by separating local component state, server data, form drafts, URL state, and persisted state.

Why it matters: Many app bugs come from stale data, lost form input, duplicated sources of truth, or state that changes in surprising places.

API integration

Fetch and display real data with loading, empty, error, retry, stale, and permission states instead of assuming the happy path.

Why it matters: Users experience the network and backend through the frontend, so the UI must explain what is happening when data is slow or unavailable.

Performance

Measure bundle size, render cost, images, network waterfalls, and interaction delays before choosing lazy loading, memoization, code splitting, or asset optimization.

Why it matters: Performance affects conversion, search visibility, and user trust. The best frontend engineers optimize based on evidence.

tools skills

React

Use React for component composition, stateful interfaces, routing, forms, and rendering product workflows from data.

Why it matters: React fluency matters when it produces readable components, clear state boundaries, and resilient UI behavior.

Vite

Use Vite for fast local development, production builds, environment variables, and frontend asset bundling.

Why it matters: Understanding the build tool helps diagnose environment issues, broken imports, asset paths, and production-only failures.

Tailwind CSS

Use Tailwind to create consistent spacing, responsive layouts, states, and component styling without scattered one-off CSS.

Why it matters: Utility-first CSS is valuable when it supports consistency, not when it becomes random class soup.

Chrome DevTools

Use DevTools to inspect layout, network requests, console errors, performance traces, storage, accessibility, and mobile behavior.

Why it matters: Debugging in the browser is a daily frontend skill, especially when the bug only appears with real DOM, CSS, and network conditions.

Storybook

Use Storybook to document component states, variants, empty/error/loading behavior, and design system usage.

Why it matters: It makes UI reviewable without clicking through the whole app and helps teams prevent component drift.

Lighthouse

Use Lighthouse as a starting audit for performance, accessibility, SEO, and best-practice issues.

Why it matters: It gives measurable feedback, but strong engineers know to pair it with real user flows and manual accessibility checks.

soft skills

Product judgment

Ask what the user is trying to do, what failure states matter, and which interaction should be fastest.

Why it matters: Frontend work is where technical choices become user experience.

Design collaboration

Translate Figma intent into usable UI while flagging missing states, impossible layouts, and accessibility gaps early.

Why it matters: Good frontend engineers improve designs through implementation feedback, not silent pixel matching.

Communication

Explain tradeoffs around scope, browser support, performance, and accessibility in plain language.

Why it matters: Clear communication keeps product, design, and engineering aligned when UI details get complex.

Debugging discipline

Reproduce issues, isolate variables, inspect real runtime behavior, and write down what changed.

Why it matters: It prevents random fixes and helps teammates trust the solution.

Tools to practise

  • React
  • Vite
  • Tailwind CSS
  • Figma
  • Storybook
  • Chrome DevTools
  • GitHub
  • Lighthouse

Projects for Frontend Engineer learners

Project 1: Build a responsive personal portfolio with project cards, contact form, and accessible navigation

Build a responsive personal portfolio with project cards, contact form, and accessible navigation.. Include realistic inputs, documented assumptions, failure handling, and evidence that the work can be reviewed by another practitioner.

Difficulty: Beginner

Technologies used

  • React
  • Vite
  • Tailwind CSS
  • Figma
  • Storybook

Skills used

  • Semantic HTML
  • CSS layout
  • JavaScript
  • accessibility
  • responsive design

Deployment suggestion: static hosting or preview environment with routed pages, analytics, error tracking, cache strategy, and rollback plan. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply Semantic HTML to a realistic user-facing web product experience.
  • Validate quality with unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling.
  • Explain tradeoffs using Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Portfolio value: Shows employers that you can deliver beginner Frontend Engineer work with realistic constraints, documentation, and reviewable evidence.

Project 2: Build a job application tracker with filters, notes, status updates, and local draft persistence

Build a job application tracker with filters, notes, status updates, and local draft persistence.. Include realistic inputs, documented assumptions, failure handling, and evidence that the work can be reviewed by another practitioner.

Difficulty: Intermediate

Technologies used

  • Vite
  • Tailwind CSS
  • Figma
  • Storybook
  • Chrome DevTools

Skills used

  • CSS layout
  • JavaScript
  • accessibility
  • responsive design
  • state management

Deployment suggestion: static hosting or preview environment with routed pages, analytics, error tracking, cache strategy, and rollback plan. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply CSS layout to a realistic user-facing web product experience.
  • Validate quality with unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling.
  • Explain tradeoffs using Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Portfolio value: Shows employers that you can deliver intermediate Frontend Engineer work with realistic constraints, documentation, and reviewable evidence.

Project 3: Create a small design system with buttons, inputs, cards, tabs, and documented states

Create a small design system with buttons, inputs, cards, tabs, and documented states.. Include realistic inputs, documented assumptions, failure handling, and evidence that the work can be reviewed by another practitioner.

Difficulty: Advanced

Technologies used

  • Tailwind CSS
  • Figma
  • Storybook
  • Chrome DevTools
  • GitHub

Skills used

  • JavaScript
  • accessibility
  • responsive design
  • state management
  • API integration

Deployment suggestion: static hosting or preview environment with routed pages, analytics, error tracking, cache strategy, and rollback plan. Publish the code with environment setup, sample data or fixtures, and a short operational note.

Learning outcomes

  • Apply JavaScript to a realistic user-facing web product experience.
  • Validate quality with unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling.
  • Explain tradeoffs using Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Portfolio value: Shows employers that you can deliver advanced Frontend Engineer work with realistic constraints, documentation, and reviewable evidence.

Learning roadmap

Beginner - 4 weeks

Topics to learn

  • semantic HTML
  • CSS layout
  • JavaScript fundamentals
  • Git basics

Checklist

  • Use semantic landmarks
  • Build responsive grid
  • Handle form validation
  • Deploy the site to static hosting
  • Learn: semantic HTML, CSS layout, JavaScript fundamentals, Git basics
  • Practise with: VS Code, Chrome DevTools, GitHub, MDN
  • Build: Build a responsive personal portfolio with project cards, contact form, and accessible navigation.
  • Validate with: unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling

Tools

  • VS Code
  • Chrome DevTools
  • GitHub
  • MDN

Project: Build a responsive personal portfolio with project cards, contact form, and accessible navigation.

Outcome: You can build clean static interfaces that behave well on mobile and desktop.

Next action: Write what you validated, what failed, and what you will improve next.

Intermediate - 5 weeks

Topics to learn

  • React components
  • props and state
  • forms
  • API fetching

Checklist

  • Create reusable components
  • Fetch API data
  • Persist local state
  • Design empty and error states
  • Learn: React components, props and state, forms, API fetching
  • Practise with: React, Vite, Tailwind CSS, REST APIs
  • Build: Build a job application tracker with filters, notes, status updates, and local draft persistence.
  • Validate with: unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling

Tools

  • React
  • Vite
  • Tailwind CSS
  • REST APIs

Project: Build a job application tracker with filters, notes, status updates, and local draft persistence.

Outcome: You can create interactive screens with real data flow and predictable states.

Next action: Write what you validated, what failed, and what you will improve next.

Advanced - 9 weeks

Topics to learn

  • accessibility
  • performance
  • component systems
  • testing basics
  • portfolio case studies
  • code review practice
  • frontend interview prep
  • ATS CV positioning

Checklist

  • Audit keyboard navigation
  • Measure Lighthouse score
  • Write component tests
  • Document component states
  • Learn: accessibility, performance, component systems, testing basics
  • Practise with: Storybook, Lighthouse, React Testing Library, Figma
  • Build: Create a small design system with buttons, inputs, cards, tabs, and documented states.
  • Validate with: unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling

Tools

  • Storybook
  • Lighthouse
  • React Testing Library
  • Figma
  • GitHub
  • Netlify
  • Career2Day CV Builder
  • LinkedIn

Project: Create a small design system with buttons, inputs, cards, tabs, and documented states.

Outcome: Your UI work becomes reusable, measurable, accessible, and easier for teams to maintain. You also have portfolio evidence, CV-ready stories, and a delivery plan for real applications.

Next action: Prepare a portfolio demo, CV bullets, and interview story from this work.

Interview preparation

Interview question 1: Semantic HTML and accessibility

Why would you choose a native button element instead of a div with an onClick handler?

Short answer: A native button already supports keyboard activation, focus, disabled state, semantics, and screen reader expectations.

Detailed answer: Use a real button when the user is triggering an action. It works with Enter and Space by default, appears correctly in the accessibility tree, supports disabled behavior, and is easier to test. A div with an onClick handler forces you to recreate browser behavior manually and is easy to get wrong. Custom elements are only justified when the native element cannot represent the interaction.

Common mistake: Adding role="button" to a div but forgetting keyboard support and visible focus styles.

Real-world example: A filter drawer toggle should be a button because it changes UI state. A link should be reserved for navigation to a new URL.

Interview question 2: Responsive layout

How would you make a card grid work on mobile, tablet, and desktop without layout jumps?

Short answer: Use content-aware grid rules, stable spacing, and predictable card dimensions rather than fixed desktop widths.

Detailed answer: Start with a single-column mobile layout, then add breakpoints where the content naturally has room for more columns. Use CSS Grid with minmax, consistent gaps, and max-width containers. Avoid fixed heights unless the design requires them, and test long titles, empty states, and real data. If images or charts are involved, define aspect ratios so loading content does not shift the page.

Common mistake: Designing a desktop grid first and shrinking it until text overflows or buttons wrap badly.

Real-world example: A careers grid can be one column on phones, two columns on tablets, and three or four on desktop, with each card keeping the same action placement.

Interview question 3: React component design

What makes a React component reusable without becoming too abstract?

Short answer: It has one clear responsibility, receives data through explicit props, exposes needed states, and avoids knowing too much about the page.

Detailed answer: A reusable component should solve a real repeated UI problem, not every possible future case. Keep data fetching and routing decisions in the page when possible, and let the component handle display, events, and visual states. Good props are specific enough to be understandable. If a component needs many boolean flags or page-specific branches, it is often better to split it into smaller components.

Common mistake: Creating a generic mega-component that supports every layout but is harder to understand than duplicated markup.

Real-world example: A CareerCard should receive name, category, salary label, skills, and link. It should not know the entire search page state.

Interview question 4: Forms and API states

A contact form submission fails after the user writes a long message. What should the UI do?

Short answer: Keep the user input, show a clear error, allow retry, and avoid making the user guess whether the message was sent.

Detailed answer: Never clear the form until the submission succeeds. Disable duplicate submits while the request is in progress, then show a specific failure message if the server or network fails. Preserve the draft in component state or local storage for important forms. If the backend returns validation errors, attach them to the relevant fields. The goal is to protect user effort and make recovery obvious.

Common mistake: Resetting the form immediately on submit, then showing a generic "something went wrong" message.

Real-world example: A Formspree contact form should show required fields, keep the message on failed submit, and communicate success only after Formspree accepts it.

Interview question 5: Performance

A React dashboard feels slow. What would you measure before optimizing?

Short answer: Measure bundle size, network waterfall, render timing, expensive components, image weight, and interaction delay in the actual user flow.

Detailed answer: Start with evidence. Use DevTools Performance and Network panels, Lighthouse, and React Profiler where appropriate. Check whether the bottleneck is JavaScript download, API latency, unnecessary re-renders, large images, expensive charts, or layout work. Then choose the smallest fix: code splitting, caching, virtualization, image compression, memoization, or backend changes. Memoizing everything before measuring often hides the real problem.

Common mistake: Using React.memo everywhere without checking whether rendering is actually the bottleneck.

Real-world example: A CV builder preview may need lazy loading for PDF/export libraries, while a careers list may only need smaller images and fewer repeated calculations.

Interview question 6: Design systems

How would you design a button component for a team design system?

Short answer: Define variants, sizes, disabled/loading states, accessible labels, focus styles, and composition rules before spreading it across the app.

Detailed answer: A design system button should map to product needs: primary, secondary, destructive, ghost, icon-only, and loading states if the app uses them. It should preserve native button behavior, allow links only through an intentional composition pattern, and provide visible focus. Document examples in Storybook with long labels and mobile widths. The component should make the common path easy and the unsafe path hard.

Common mistake: Styling a div to look like a button or creating separate button styles in every feature.

Real-world example: An icon-only save button needs an accessible name, tooltip if needed, a stable square size, and clear disabled/loading behavior.

Interview question 7: Frontend security

What frontend security issues should you consider when rendering user-generated content?

Short answer: Avoid unsafe HTML, rely on escaping, sanitize trusted-rich text, protect tokens, and assume API data can be malformed.

Detailed answer: React escapes normal text values, which helps prevent many XSS issues. Risk returns when using dangerouslySetInnerHTML, rendering markdown, injecting third-party scripts, or storing sensitive tokens carelessly. If rich text is required, sanitize it with a trusted library and restrict allowed tags. Also validate URLs, avoid exposing secrets in frontend environment variables, and handle malformed API responses gracefully.

Common mistake: Assuming all backend data is safe because it came from your own API.

Real-world example: A blog page that renders rich article content should sanitize HTML and avoid allowing arbitrary script, iframe, or event-handler attributes.

Interview question 8: Production debugging

Users report that a page works locally but breaks after deployment. How do you investigate?

Short answer: Compare build output, environment variables, asset paths, network requests, console errors, server routing, and browser-specific behavior.

Detailed answer: Production failures often come from differences in build mode, base paths, missing environment variables, cached assets, server rewrite rules, or API URLs. First reproduce against the deployed URL and collect console/network errors. Check whether JS and CSS assets load, whether routes fall back to index.html, and whether the API target is reachable. Then compare the production build locally with preview mode before changing code.

Common mistake: Changing random React code before checking whether Nginx, assets, or environment configuration is the real cause.

Real-world example: A Vite app may work locally but show a blank page on a VPS if Nginx serves the wrong folder or does not route nested paths to index.html.

Open all Frontend Engineer interview questions

Quiz questions

Quiz question 1: semantic HTML and accessibility

What is the best first accessibility choice for a Frontend Engineer? Consider this during local development. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Use ARIA roles on every div.
  • Remove focus styles for a cleaner visual design.
  • Use only color to show validation errors.
  • Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.

Correct answer: Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.

Explanation: Native elements bring keyboard behavior, semantics, and browser support by default. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 2: React component design

What makes a React component easier to reuse? Consider this during code review. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Hardcoding copy and API URLs.
  • Putting all page logic into one component.
  • Clear props, limited responsibility, and predictable rendering states.
  • Reading every value from global state.

Correct answer: Clear props, limited responsibility, and predictable rendering states.

Explanation: Reusable components have clean boundaries and do not know too much about their parent page. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 3: state and forms

What should a robust form handle besides happy-path typing? Consider this after a failed deployment. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Only the final API response.
  • Validation, loading, errors, empty states, and draft persistence when useful.
  • Only the submit button style.
  • Only desktop screen sizes.

Correct answer: Validation, loading, errors, empty states, and draft persistence when useful.

Explanation: Real users pause, make mistakes, lose network, and return later. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 4: API integration

What is a production-ready data fetching state? Consider this while debugging production-like data. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.
  • It assumes the network always succeeds.
  • It blocks the entire app for one request.
  • It logs errors only to the console.

Correct answer: It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.

Explanation: Data fetching is part of the user experience, not only a technical detail. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 5: performance

What should you do before performance optimization? Consider this when explaining the work to a hiring manager. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Rewrite the app in a new framework immediately.
  • Memoize every component.
  • Remove all animations from every page.
  • Measure the bottleneck with real tools and realistic user conditions.

Correct answer: Measure the bottleneck with real tools and realistic user conditions.

Explanation: Measurement prevents time spent optimizing the wrong thing. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 6: responsive product design

What is the best responsive layout habit? Consider this while reducing operational risk. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • Hide important actions on mobile.
  • Use fixed pixel widths everywhere.
  • Design stable layout rules for common content lengths across mobile and desktop.
  • Scale all font sizes with viewport width.

Correct answer: Design stable layout rules for common content lengths across mobile and desktop.

Explanation: Responsive UI needs planned constraints, not accidental shrinking. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 7: Semantic HTML

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses Semantic HTML. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this when choosing between two tools. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.
  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.
  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.
  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.

Correct answer: A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: Semantic HTML becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 8: CSS layout

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses CSS layout. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this during a security or privacy review. Difficulty focus: beginner.

Answer choices

  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.
  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.
  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.
  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.

Correct answer: A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: CSS layout becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses JavaScript. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this while improving performance. Difficulty focus: beginner.

  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.
  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.
  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.
  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: JavaScript becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Explanation: accessibility becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 11: API integration

What is a production-ready data fetching state? Consider this after a failed deployment. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Answer choices

  • It blocks the entire app for one request.
  • It logs errors only to the console.
  • It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.
  • It assumes the network always succeeds.

Correct answer: It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.

Explanation: Data fetching is part of the user experience, not only a technical detail. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 12: performance

What should you do before performance optimization? Consider this while debugging production-like data. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Answer choices

  • Remove all animations from every page.
  • Measure the bottleneck with real tools and realistic user conditions.
  • Rewrite the app in a new framework immediately.
  • Memoize every component.

Correct answer: Measure the bottleneck with real tools and realistic user conditions.

Explanation: Measurement prevents time spent optimizing the wrong thing. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 13: responsive product design

What is the best responsive layout habit? Consider this when explaining the work to a hiring manager. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Answer choices

  • Design stable layout rules for common content lengths across mobile and desktop.
  • Scale all font sizes with viewport width.
  • Hide important actions on mobile.
  • Use fixed pixel widths everywhere.

Correct answer: Design stable layout rules for common content lengths across mobile and desktop.

Explanation: Responsive UI needs planned constraints, not accidental shrinking. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses Semantic HTML. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this while reducing operational risk. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Quiz question 19: state management

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses state management. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this during local development. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Answer choices

  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.
  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.
  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.
  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.

Correct answer: A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: state management becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 20: semantic HTML and accessibility

What is the best first accessibility choice for a Frontend Engineer? Consider this during code review. Difficulty focus: intermediate.

Answer choices

  • Use only color to show validation errors.
  • Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.
  • Use ARIA roles on every div.
  • Remove focus styles for a cleaner visual design.

Correct answer: Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.

Explanation: Native elements bring keyboard behavior, semantics, and browser support by default. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 21: Semantic HTML

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses Semantic HTML. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this when explaining the work to a hiring manager. Difficulty focus: advanced.

  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.
  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.
  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.
  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.

Correct answer: A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: Semantic HTML becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 26: state management

A Frontend Engineer portfolio uses state management. What would make it credible to an interviewer? Consider this before publishing the portfolio project. Difficulty focus: advanced.

Answer choices

  • A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.
  • A copied tutorial with renamed variables and no setup notes.
  • A tool list with no finished workflow or evidence.
  • A definition memorized without showing operational consequences.

Correct answer: A working artifact with realistic inputs, documented tradeoffs, unit tests, UI automation, accessibility checks, cross-browser testing, and performance profiling, and clear impact on Core Web Vitals, task completion, accessibility issues, bundle size, render cost, and conversion.

Explanation: state management becomes job-ready when it is connected to implementation, verification, deployment, and role-specific impact. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 27: semantic HTML and accessibility

What is the best first accessibility choice for a Frontend Engineer? Consider this during local development. Difficulty focus: advanced.

Answer choices

  • Use ARIA roles on every div.
  • Remove focus styles for a cleaner visual design.
  • Use only color to show validation errors.
  • Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.

Correct answer: Use native semantic elements before adding custom ARIA.

Explanation: Native elements bring keyboard behavior, semantics, and browser support by default. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 28: React component design

What makes a React component easier to reuse? Consider this during code review. Difficulty focus: advanced.

Answer choices

  • Hardcoding copy and API URLs.
  • Putting all page logic into one component.
  • Clear props, limited responsibility, and predictable rendering states.
  • Reading every value from global state.

Correct answer: Clear props, limited responsibility, and predictable rendering states.

Explanation: Reusable components have clean boundaries and do not know too much about their parent page. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 29: state and forms

What should a robust form handle besides happy-path typing? Consider this after a failed deployment. Difficulty focus: advanced.

Answer choices

  • Only the final API response.
  • Validation, loading, errors, empty states, and draft persistence when useful.
  • Only the submit button style.
  • Only desktop screen sizes.

Correct answer: Validation, loading, errors, empty states, and draft persistence when useful.

Explanation: Real users pause, make mistakes, lose network, and return later. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Quiz question 30: API integration

What is a production-ready data fetching state? Consider this while debugging production-like data. Difficulty focus: advanced.

Answer choices

  • It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.
  • It assumes the network always succeeds.
  • It blocks the entire app for one request.
  • It logs errors only to the console.

Correct answer: It handles loading, success, empty, error, retry, and stale data behavior.

Explanation: Data fetching is part of the user experience, not only a technical detail. This is why the scenario matters for Frontend Engineer work.

Open Frontend Engineer quiz practice

Related careers

  • Analytics Engineer
  • Solutions Architect
  • Enterprise Architect
  • Software Architect
  • Backend Engineer

Frequently asked questions

How long does it take to become a Frontend Engineer?

This Frontend Engineer roadmap is structured for about 18 focused weeks of learning, projects, interview practice and CV preparation.

What tools should a Frontend Engineer learn?

Frontend Engineer learners should practise tools such as React, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Figma, Storybook, Chrome DevTools, GitHub, Lighthouse.

What projects should I build for Frontend Engineer roles?

Build projects that prove role-specific skills, such as Build a responsive personal portfolio with project cards, contact form, and accessible navigation, Build a job application tracker with filters, notes, status updates, and local draft persistence, Create a small design system with buttons, inputs, cards, tabs, and documented states.

Contact Us
